column3: In Objective 1.2, USB is a common wired connection family for mobile charging and data transfer.
column4:
Memory Trick

The acronym is broad because USB describes a connection family, not just one connector shape.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.2; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2

column2: USB-C is a {{c1::reversible}} connector that can be inserted either way.
column3: USB-C is a connector shape. Supported signals and features still depend on the device and adapter.
column4:
Exam Trap

Reversible connector shape is not the same as universal support for every USB-C feature.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.2; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2
column9: A+::220-1201::1.2 A+::220-1201::ConnectingMobileDevices Cloze HighYield USB Connector

column2: {{c1::Lightning}} is the Apple proprietary mobile connector identified for iPhone and iPad devices in Objective 1.2.
column3: OpenAPlus does not infer current support for every Apple model from this objective-level fact.
column4:
Source Note

Use this as an exam-scope connector association, not as a current purchasing or repair guarantee.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.2; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2

column2: NFC stands for {{c1::Near Field Communication}} and is used for very short-range wireless exchange.
column3: Typical Objective 1.2 examples include payment, transportation, in-person information exchange, and access-token workflows.
column4:
Exam Trap

NFC is about close proximity. It is not the best answer for a headset, speaker, or smartwatch connection.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.2; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2
column9: A+::220-1201::1.2 A+::220-1201::ConnectingMobileDevices Cloze HighYield NFC Acronym

column2: Bluetooth is commonly used for a short-range {{c1::personal area network}} connection.
column3: Mobile examples include headsets, headphones, smartwatches, speakers, health monitors, and automobile integration.
column4:
Memory Trick

Bluetooth is the “nearby peripherals” answer when the connection must persist beyond a tap.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.3; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2
column9: A+::220-1201::1.2 A+::220-1201::ConnectingMobileDevices Cloze HighYield Bluetooth PAN

column2: Hotspot/tethering shares a phone's {{c1::cellular data}} connection with other devices.
column3: Availability and cost can depend on the phone, provider, and cellular plan.
column4:
Real Help Desk

If tethering fails, verify the plan and provider restrictions before treating it as a laptop Wi-Fi problem.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.3; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2

column2: A 3.5 mm {{c1::TRRS}} headset connector can carry analog audio for earphones and a microphone.
column4:
Exam Trap

The extra contact matters because a headset needs a microphone path, not only stereo output.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.3; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2

column2: A port replicator is similar to a docking station, but it commonly lacks {{c1::expansion card}} capability.
column3: Port replicators are usually simpler connectivity expanders, often connected by USB.
column4:
Exam Trap

If the question emphasizes adding desktop-style expansion hardware, a docking station is usually the stronger answer.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.3; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.2
